data.title: Shenandoah Telecommunications announces CEO transition
data.description:
• Shenandoah Telecommunications (NASDAQ:SHEN) has named Edward McKay as its next president and chief executive officer, effective September 1, 2025.
  • Current CEO Christopher E. French will transition to executive chairman, remaining active in company strategy and leadership.
  • McKay currently serves as executive vice president and chief operating officer.

data.title: Titan International outlines $450M–$475M Q3 revenue guidance amid Roderos investment and consumer rebound signals
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Titan International (TWI) Q2 2025

Management View

  • CEO Paul George Reitz indicated the Titan team delivered a "solid quarter" with results "within our guidance ranges for both revenue and adjusted EBITDA while also driving positive free cash flow for the quarter." Reitz noted ongoing market softness and buyers' caution, attributing hesitancy to "waiting for interest rates to come down" and uncertainty about tariffs and trade policy. He pointed out that "trade policy applied somewhat consistently around the globe would benefit Titan in the long term," emphasizing Titan’s competitive positioning when "irrational import pricing is removed from the equation."
  • Reitz announced a "minority investment in a strategic partnership with Brazilian wheel manufacturer, Roderos," stating, "We have been actively looking for an opportunity to get into the Brazilian wheel market for a number of years and this is a great partnership to do so." He described Brazil as an "increasingly important market for Titan" and expects the transaction to close in the third quarter, subject to regulatory approval.
  • Reitz highlighted progress with the expanded Goodyear licensing agreement, noting, "We are busy working to maximize this opportunity with the Goodyear brand...and we think it will absolutely help drive growth over time."
  • CFO David A. Martin stated, "Our results in the second quarter were in line with our expectations as revenues were $461 million with adjusted EBITDA of $30 million. We were also able to drive positive cash flow in the quarter of $4 million."
  • Martin added, "On a sequential basis, our gross margins improved 100 basis points from 14% in the first quarter with product mix being the main reason for the sequential improvement. Looking at margins by segment in the quarter, all 3 showed expansion versus the first quarter."

Outlook

  • Martin provided Q3 2025 guidance of "revenues of $450 million to $475 million, adjusted EBITDA of $25 million to $30 million and I also expect to see tax expense of around $4 million to $5 million, which is similar to our Q2 level." He stated, "The midpoints of our revenue and adjusted EBITDA guidance imply growth in both metrics when compared to Q3 of last year as well as relatively flat performance versus Q2."
  • Management expects "the consumer segment will rebound a bit as channel inventories have gotten too low."
  • There was no comparison to analysts' estimates as this data was not available.

Financial Results

  • Revenues for Q2 were $461 million and adjusted EBITDA was $30 million.
  • The company "drove positive cash flow in the quarter of $4 million."
  • Gross margins improved to "15%" from "14%" in the first quarter, with product mix cited as the primary driver. Margins by segment: Ag at "14.6% compared to 12.4% in the first quarter," EMC at "11.5% versus 10.4%," and consumer at "20.4% compared to 19.6%."
  • SG&A expense was $52 million or "around 11% of sales," up about 1.5% from last year due to labor cost inflation.
  • R&D expenses were $4.3 million, "not a big change year-over-year."
  • Net debt at quarter-end declined $10 million from the first quarter to $401 million.
  • Income tax expense was $4.7 million with an "effective rate well over 100%."

Q&A

  • Michael Shlisky, D.A. Davidson: Asked about Q3 sales and EBITDA guidance. Martin explained that "we normally have our seasonal shutdowns of plant and equipment for the summer maintenance schedules as well as the holiday schedules in Europe. Overall, the product mix we don't think it's going to be quite the same as what we saw in Q2."
  • Shlisky inquired about the ag sector and potential for improvement in the U.S. Reitz replied, "there's an overall tone that's just pretty quiet...that quietness...just means right now there's definitely a pause to wait and see obviously what's going on...with some of the tariff deadlines and then any Fed action later in the year."
  • Shlisky asked about U.S. NOLs. Martin responded, "there are some NOLs that could potentially hit valuation allowances needed if we don't see market conditions improve...but...cash taxes...have been relatively stable."
  • Stephen Ferazani, Sidoti: Questioned consumer segment gross margin performance amid tariffs. Martin clarified, "the sequential improvement in margins was a little bit to do with just product mix, a different OEM versus aftermarket...we're managing the tariff cost with moderation of prices accordingly. But it was not a material impact."
  • Ferazani asked about the decline in consumer. Martin replied, "there was some pause in the market during Q2 for, call it, tariff impact, just the wait-and-see aspect and nobody wanting to hold inventory...that's why we're starting to see some recovery and some improvement in early Q3 because their inventories just got too low."
  • Derek Soderberg, Cantor Fitzgerald: Inquired about the Japan trade deal, depreciation legislation, and their impacts. Reitz said, "where we're seeing these tariff agreements settle is at a level that I would say is net positive for our business...but both segments tie back to one thin, it's just interest rates. We still see interest rates being that cloudy factor that's hanging out over making purchases and holding inventory."
  • Soderberg also asked about the Roderos deal. Reitz explained, "we'll start off with an initial minority investment of $4 million for 20% and then we do have some opportunities in the future to see that grow and expand."
  • Joseph Gomes, NOBLE Capital: Asked for clarity around consumer inventory and potential rebound. Reitz responded, "some of our customers had paused in Q2...they're now realizing that the retail pull-through demand has remained stronger than anticipated and they're building back their inventory."
  • Gomes asked about third-party sourcing and military market. Reitz responded, "we are going to continue to be that one-stop shop...Military, we see the opportunities...I'm investing my time and so will my team."
  • Gomes inquired about LSW penetration. Reitz stated, "we're working on is the marketing towards those midsize farms...if we have the marketing plus the financing package in today's world, I think we can really kick those sales into gear."
  • Kirk Ludtke, Imperial Capital: Asked about further investment in Roderos. Reitz noted, "not beyond that initial $4 million."
  • Ludtke asked about timing for the Roderos deal to move the needle. Reitz said, "it's what we can do with our product portfolio together...now we can sell wheels and tires assembled to the OEMs."
  • Ludtke questioned customer reactions to tariffs. Reitz answered, "we have had customers ask us about our capacity for next year...feeling that we're well positioned to service their needs for '26, but customers are just not talking a lot about '26 at this point."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed a neutral to slightly positive tone, seeking clarification on the impact of tariffs, segment performance, and future opportunities, with a focus on inventory trends and strategic moves.
  • Management maintained a confident and proactive tone in both prepared remarks and responses, with Reitz frequently reiterating Titan’s readiness: "we are really doing quite well and holding our own despite some significant macro challenges thus far in 2025."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, the overall analyst tone shifted from cautious to more neutral, while management’s confidence remained steady.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q2 guidance and results were within expectations, following a Q1 that had results "at the higher end of our guidance ranges."
  • Revenue declined from Q1’s $491 million to Q2’s $461 million, while adjusted EBITDA decreased from $31 million to $30 million.
  • Gross margins improved sequentially, with all segments expanding versus Q1.
  • Strategic initiatives advanced, notably the Roderos partnership and continued focus on Goodyear licensing.
  • Analysts in both quarters focused on tariffs, inventory, and market sentiment, but Q2 saw more attention to consumer segment dynamics and international expansion.
  • Management’s tone remained confident, emphasizing operational agility and readiness for market rebounds in both quarters.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management highlighted persistent market softness, with OEMs and end markets "remain[ing] similar to last quarter as buyers of equipment continue to take a wait-and-see approach."
  • Interest rates and tariffs were repeatedly cited as primary headwinds for demand and purchasing activity.
  • Elevated tax rates due to geographic profit distribution were noted as a near-term challenge.
  • Channel inventory levels, particularly in the consumer segment, presented a risk but are already showing signs of correction.
  • Analysts probed for potential financial constraints; management assured that leverage is at its peak and covenants are not a concern.

Final Takeaway

Titan International delivered a quarter in line with expectations, maintaining operational discipline and advancing strategic initiatives such as the Roderos partnership and expanded Goodyear licensing. Management emphasized agility in responding to ongoing market uncertainties, particularly around tariffs and interest rates, while highlighting early signs of recovery in consumer demand. The outlook for the next quarter projects stable revenues, with continued focus on cost management and readiness to capitalize on rebounding demand as macro headwinds abate.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Boot Barn's strong FQ1 results leads to upgraded guidance
data.description:
Shares of Boot Barn (NYSE:BOOT) are rallying in Thursday’s after hours trading as fiscal first quarter results, including comparable store sales, were better than expectations, leading the retailer to raise its full year sales guidance.

“With our four strategic initiatives delivering consistent results and the opportunity we have to double our store count, we remain confident in our ability to continue generating value for our shareholders over the long term,” said CEO John Hazen.

Led by an increase in sales and merchandise margin, which compensated for the cost of new stores, Boot Barn (NYSE:BOOT) earned a profit of $1.74 per share, up from $1.26 a year ago and $0.20 better than expected. Total sales were up 19% to $504.1M, nearly $9M better than expected. The company’s profit margin improved to 39.1%, a 210 basis point gain from the same quarter last year. This resulted from a 180 basis point increase in merchandise margin rate and 30 basis points of leverage in buying, occupancy and distribution center costs.

With retail same store sales up 9.5% and e-commerce sales up 9.3%, same store sales across all channels increased 9.4%, a significant improvement from +1.4% last year and above the consensus estimate of +6.77%.

For the current fiscal quarter, Boot Barn (NYSE:BOOT) expects to earn a profit between $1.19 and $1.27 per share, with the midpoint of $1.23 below the consensus estimate. However, sales of $487M to $495M is above estimates of $481.86M.

Same store sales growth is pegged for 4.5% to 6.5% versus +2.32% estimates.

For FY26, the company is projecting a profit of $5.80 to $6.70 per share, versus earlier estimates of $5.50 to $6.40. This is compared to $6.27 estimates. Sales guidance was raised to a range of $2.10B and $2.18B from $2.07B to $2.15B, straddling the $2.15B estimate.

Same store sales for FY26 are projected to be down 0.5% to up 3.5% versus +1.85% estimates.

During the fiscal year, Boot Barn (BOOT) expects to open between 65 and 70 new stores.

data.title: Aecon reports Q2 results
data.description:
• Aecon press release (OTCPK:AEGXF): Q2 Revenue of $1.3B beats by $499.53M.
  • Operating profit of $2.3 million for the three months ended June 30, 2025 compared to an operating loss of $166.3 million for the same period in 2024.
  • Loss attributable to shareholders of $7.6 million (diluted loss per share of $0.12).
  • Reported backlog at June 30, 2025 of $10,746 million compared to backlog of $6,186 million at June 30, 2024. 

  • OUTLOOK

    Revenue in 2025 is expected to be stronger than 2024, driven by record reported backlog of $10.7 billion at the end of the second quarter of 2025, recurring revenue programs continuing to see solid demand, a strong bid pipeline, and the impact of strategic acquisitions completed in the second half of 2024. Revenue growth is expected in most of the Construction sectors.

data.title: Cellnex Telecom, S.A. reports 1H results
data.description:
• Cellnex Telecom, S.A. press release (OTCPK:CLNXF): 1H revenues increased +6% and EBITDAaL +8.1%.
  • Recurring Leveraged Free Cash Flow (RLFCF) rose to €832 million, compared to €781 million last year that represents a +6.5% increase.
  • Reported revenues reached €1,942 million, compared to €1,921 million in the same period of the previous year, representing a +1.1% increase, impacted by the change of permitter of Austria and Ireland.
  • EBITDAaL (EBITDA after leases) stood at €1,157 million, up +3.8% from €1,114 million in H1 2024.
  • Cellnex reiterates its 2025 guidance: Revenues between €3,950 and €4,050 million, Adjusted EBITDA between €3,275 and €3,375 million, RLFCF between €1,900 and €1,950 million and FCF between €280 and €380 million.

data.title: BWX Technologies names new CFO
data.description:
• BWX Technologies (NYSE:BWXT) has appointed Mike T. Fitzgerald as chief financial officer, following his tenure as interim CFO since May 12.
  • Fitzgerald joined BWXT in 2022 as VP of Finance and chief accounting officer.
  • Source: Press release
